summary: DeVault argues that accessibility is far broader than ARIA tags and screen readers, encompassing economic accessibility, localization, intuitiveness, and resource efficiency. He criticizes developers for treating accessibility as a checklist of band-aids applied to fundamentally inaccessible designs rather than building with empathy from the ground up. The post frames Electron apps consuming 8GB of RAM, huge JavaScript bundles, and data-hungry mobile apps as accessibility failures that disproportionately tax poorer users. He extends the concept to include localization, unfamiliar design language, and deliberately addictive social media patterns. DeVault acknowledges that free software is 'pretty pathetic' at accessibility, attributing it to laziness and building software only for people like its authors. He calls for designing simple, intuitive software as the foundation of accessibility rather than retrofitting unusable software with half-measures.
key_points: Accessibility extends far beyond screen readers to include economic accessibility, localization, intuitiveness, and resource efficiency
Most developers treat accessibility as a checklist of band-aids rather than a foundational design principle
Resource-hungry software like Electron apps effectively charges poorer users a tax, making bloat an accessibility issue
Free software is poor at accessibility not due to perverse profit incentives but due to laziness and inability to design for people unlike its authors
Simple, intuitive software is the foundation of genuine accessibility, not retroactive patches on fundamentally unusable designs
money_quotes: The industry's relationship with accessibility borders on willful ignorance.

Instead of designing for people like you and then patching it up until it's semi-functional for people who are not like you, a wise developer places themselves into the shoes of the person they're designing for.
When your Electron crapware app eats up 8G of RAM, it may be fine on your 32G developer workstation, but not so much for someone who cannot afford anything other than a used $50 laptop from eBay.
You're essentially charging poorer users a tax to use your website.
Ads are inaccessible by design, but good luck explaining that to your boss.
Simple, intuitive software is the foundation of accessibility which works for everyone, including you and me — and not retroactively adding half-assed tools to fundamentally unusable software.
The model of wasteful consumerism required to keep up with modern software is destroying the planet.
Free software is pretty pathetic when it comes to accessibility.
key_insight: Genuine accessibility requires designing simple, intuitive, resource-efficient software from the ground up rather than applying band-aids to bloated, fundamentally inaccessible designs.
